steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary duct obstruction; gallstone pancreatitis with biliary component; complex peripancreatic/pericholecystic collection
ensemble.information_gap: The outside abdominal ultrasound showed gallstones and a 'complex collection in the region of the gallbladder or head of pancreas' — the treating physician needs a higher-quality, dedicated hepatobiliary ultrasound at this institution to characterize that collection, assess the common bile duct for dilation or stones, and evaluate the gallbladder wall for acute cholecystitis, given markedly elevated transaminases (AST 800, ALT 634), elevated lipase (2238), rising lactate (3.8→4.7), and the ERCP fellow already being consulted.
ensemble.expected_finding: Common bile duct dilation (suggesting choledocholithiasis driving the transaminase elevation and possibly the pancreatitis), pericholecystic fluid or gallbladder wall thickening consistent with acute cholecystitis, or better characterization of the complex peripancreatic/pericholecystic collection seen on outside imaging.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The outside ultrasound identified a 'complex collection in the region of the gallbladder or head of pancreas' and gallstones, but was performed at an outside facility; a dedicated single-organ hepatobiliary ultrasound at this institution is needed to characterize the CBD, gallbladder, and collection before ERCP planning proceeds.
grounding: History: 'ABD US with gallstones and complex collection in the region of the gallbladder or head of pancreas'
Physical exam: 'moderate epigastric and upper abdominal tenderness RUQ > LUQ'
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has biochemical evidence of gallstone pancreatitis (lipase >2000, transaminases in the hundreds, mildly elevated bilirubin, gallstones on outside US) with a rising lactate and a complex collection near the gallbladder/pancreatic head seen on outside imaging. Before the ERCP fellow can proceed, I need a dedicated hepatobiliary ultrasound at this institution to determine whether the CBD is dilated (confirming choledocholithiasis as the driver), whether the gallbladder shows acute cholecystitis, and to better define the nature of that complex collection — all of which will directly inform the urgency and approach of ERCP.

grounding: History: 'ABD US with gallstones and complex collection in the region of the gallbladder or head of pancreas'
Physical exam: 'moderate epigastric and upper abdominal tenderness RUQ > LUQ'
representative_ex_ante.reasoning: This patient has biochemical evidence of gallstone pancreatitis (lipase >2000, transaminases in the hundreds, mildly elevated bilirubin, gallstones on outside US) with a rising lactate and a complex collection near the gallbladder/pancreatic head seen on outside imaging. Before the ERCP fellow can proceed, I need a dedicated hepatobiliary ultrasound at this institution to determine whether the CBD is dilated (confirming choledocholithiasis as the driver), whether the gallbladder shows acute cholecystitis, and to better define the nature of that complex collection — all of which will directly inform the urgency and approach of ERCP.

verification.actual_finding: The ultrasound demonstrated mild CBD dilation (7 mm), cholelithiasis, and a heterogeneous hypoechoic region inferior to the gallbladder without internal vascularity or peristalsis, consistent with a complex peripancreatic/pericholecystic collection.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The report confirmed two of the three expected findings — mild CBD dilation suggesting choledocholithiasis and better characterization of the complex collection seen on outside imaging — though gallbladder wall thickening/acute cholecystitis could not be fully assessed due to posterior shadowing from stones.

steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Biliary obstruction/choledocholithiasis with gallstone pancreatitis; peripancreatic or pericholecystic fluid collection/abscess; pancreatic necrosis
ensemble.information_gap: The ultrasound identified a heterogeneous hypoechoic region inferior to the gallbladder of unclear origin (peripancreatic fluid collection vs. pericholecystic abscess vs. other mass), and the pancreas itself was not visualized due to overlying bowel gas — the physician cannot characterize the extent of pancreatic inflammation, necrosis, or ductal/biliary obstruction from US alone.
ensemble.expected_finding: CT is expected to delineate the pancreas fully — specifically to identify peripancreatic fluid collections, pancreatic necrosis, or hemorrhage consistent with severe/necrotizing pancreatitis — and to characterize the heterogeneous inferior gallbladder region (fluid collection, abscess, or mass), as well as assess for choledocholithiasis or biliary ductal dilation beyond the 7 mm CBD seen on US.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: With lipase >2000, markedly elevated transaminases, a rising lactate (3.8→4.7), leukocytosis, and an uncharacterized heterogeneous pericholecystic/peripancreatic collection on US with the pancreas itself not visualized, CT with contrast is the appropriate next step to stage severity of pancreatitis, identify necrosis, and define the ambiguous fluid collection.
grounding: US report: 'Overlying bowel gas obscures detailed evaluation of the pancreas'

US report: 'Inferior to the gallbladder is a heterogeneous, predominantly hypoechoic region that does not demonstrate internal vascularity and does not clearly demonstrate normal peristaltic activity seen with bowel'
US report: 'The CBD is mildly dilated and measures 7 mm'
Labs: 'White Blood Cells: 17.1 K/uL', 'Absolute Neutrophil Count: 15.2 K/uL'
History: 'ABD US with gallstones and complex collection in the region of the gallbladder or head of pancreas'
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has biochemically severe gallstone pancreatitis (lipase >2000, AST/ALT markedly elevated, rising lactate) with an uncharacterized heterogeneous collection near the gallbladder/pancreatic head on ultrasound and the pancreas itself not visualized due to bowel gas. CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the logical next step to fully characterize pancreatic parenchymal integrity (necrosis vs. edematous pancreatitis), define the nature of the pericholecystic/peripancreatic collection (fluid, abscess, hemorrhage), and assess the biliary tree — information critical for determining whether urgent ERCP, surgical, or interventional radiology involvement is needed.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: Biliary obstruction/choledocholithiasis with gallstone pancreatitis; peripancreatic or pericholecystic fluid collection/abscess; pancreatic necrosis
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: The ultrasound identified a heterogeneous hypoechoic region inferior to the gallbladder of unclear origin (peripancreatic fluid collection vs. pericholecystic abscess vs. other mass), and the pancreas itself was not visualized due to overlying bowel gas — the physician cannot characterize the extent of pancreatic inflammation, necrosis, or ductal/biliary obstruction from US alone.

grounding: US report: 'Overlying bowel gas obscures detailed evaluation of the pancreas'

US report: 'Inferior to the gallbladder is a heterogeneous, predominantly hypoechoic region that does not demonstrate internal vascularity and does not clearly demonstrate normal peristaltic activity seen with bowel'
US report: 'The CBD is mildly dilated and measures 7 mm'
Labs: 'White Blood Cells: 17.1 K/uL', 'Absolute Neutrophil Count: 15.2 K/uL'
History: 'ABD US with gallstones and complex collection in the region of the gallbladder or head of pancreas'
representative_ex_ante.reasoning: This patient has biochemically severe gallstone pancreatitis (lipase >2000, AST/ALT markedly elevated, rising lactate) with an uncharacterized heterogeneous collection near the gallbladder/pancreatic head on ultrasound and the pancreas itself not visualized due to bowel gas. CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the logical next step to fully characterize pancreatic parenchymal integrity (necrosis vs. edematous pancreatitis), define the nature of the pericholecystic/peripancreatic collection (fluid, abscess, hemorrhage), and assess the biliary tree — information critical for determining whether urgent ERCP, surgical, or interventional radiology involvement is needed.

verification.actual_finding: CT demonstrated >50% pancreatic non-enhancement consistent with necrotizing pancreatitis, a diffuse acute peripancreatic fluid collection extending into the pericholecystic space (explaining the US heterogeneous region), no biliary ductal dilation, and no gallstones.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The CT fulfilled the expected role by characterizing the pancreas as severely/necrotizing pancreatitis with peripancreatic fluid collections, clarifying the inferior gallbladder region as pericholecystic extension of the same fluid collection, and resolving the biliary question by showing no intrahepatic or extrahepatic ductal dilation despite the prior 7 mm CBD on US.
